title: Global aquaculture production and wild fish used for animal feed
subtitle:
Some wild fish catch is processed into fishmeal and oils for animal feed – this is used for land-based livestock and fish farms (aquaculture).
Efficiency improvements in aquaculture, and changes in the diets of farmed fish means production has increased rapidly, without increasing inputs from wild fish.
caption:
Global aquaculture production and wild fish used for animal feed

Some wild fish catch is processed into fishmeal and oils for animal feed – this is used for land-based livestock and fish farms (aquaculture).
Efficiency improvements in aquaculture, and changes in the diets of farmed fish means production has increased rapidly, without increasing inputs from wild fish.

This line chart tracks two global metrics from 1950 to 2015: aquaculture production and the volume of wild fish used for animal feed, measured in millions of tonnes. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 100 million tonnes. The dark blue line, representing aquaculture production, begins near zero in 1950 and remains below 10 million tonnes until the late 1980s. From approximately 1990 onward, this line exhibits a steep, continuous upward trajectory, crossing the 40 million tonne mark around the year 2000 and surpassing 100 million tonnes by the end of the timeline in 2015. In contrast, the green line, which indicates wild fish used as fishmeal and oil, displays a different pattern. Starting near zero in 1950, it rises to fluctuate primarily between 15 and 25 million tonnes from the 1960s through 2015. This green line shows significant year-to-year volatility but lacks the sustained exponential growth seen in aquaculture output. While aquaculture production climbs well above the 80 million tonne gridline, the usage of wild fish for feed remains contained within the lower section of the chart, generally staying below the 30 million tonne level after the initial rise. The visual gap between the two lines widens considerably in the final decades shown, with aquaculture production reaching values roughly four to five times higher than the volume of wild fish utilized for feed.
